slides.title: Everything Is a Number
slides.subtitle: How a machine that only knows 0 and 1 stores your photos, your music, and your name — and what it costs to squeeze the world into bits
slides.footerLeft: AP Computer Science Principles · College Board CED
slides.script: Welcome to Big Idea 2, Data. Topic 2.1 is the foundation everything else in this unit stands on: computers store and process exactly one thing, sequences of 0s and 1s, and everything you have ever seen a computer do is built from that. Today has three jobs. First, how bits represent data. Second, what it costs to represent the world with a fixed number of bits — the two famous failure modes. Third, the hands-on skill: converting between binary and decimal in both directions, which the AP exam tests directly.

slides.heading: By the End of Today You Will...

items.text: Explain how all data — numbers, text, colors, sound — can be represented using sequences of bits, and why the same bits can mean different things in different contexts (LO DAT-1.A)
Explain the two consequences of using a fixed number of bits: integer overflow and round-off error (LO DAT-1.B)
Convert a positive integer from decimal to binary and from binary to decimal — a directly tested AP skill (LO DAT-1.C)

slides.script: Three targets today, one per learning objective. First, DAT-1.A: explaining how any kind of data can be represented with bits, and the key insight that the same sequence of bits can mean completely different things depending on context. Second, DAT-1.B: what goes wrong when you only have a fixed number of bits — integer overflow and round-off errors, both of which have caused real-world disasters. Third, DAT-1.C: the mechanical skill of converting between base 2 and base 10 in both directions. The exam tests the conversion skill directly, so the back half of class is practice.

slides.prompt: You must send a friend tomorrow's temperature — but your only communication channel is a row of 8 light switches, each either ON or OFF. The temperature will be a whole number between 0 and 100.
slides.subprompt: Write 3–5 sentences: design a scheme your friend could decode. Could your 8 switches handle a temperature of 300? What about 72.5? What breaks?
slides.directions: Independent writing. Be ready to share your thinking.
slides.script: Five minutes, on your own. Eight light switches, each on or off, and you need to send a whole number between 0 and 100. Design any scheme you want, as long as your friend could decode it. Then push on it: could your switches carry 300? Could they carry 72.5? Write down exactly where your scheme breaks. Keep your answer — by the end of today you'll know that you just invented binary representation, and the two places your scheme broke are the two consequences the College Board names.

slides.heading: What You'll Be Filling In
slides.subheading: Your Guided Notes have 4 sections that match today's lesson. Green = CB required.

sections.title: Representing Data with Bits
sections.description: Bits, bytes, abstraction, and analog-to-digital sampling (EK DAT-1.A.1–A.4).

sections.title: The Cost of Finite Bits
sections.description: Integer overflow and round-off error — what they are and when they strike (EK DAT-1.B.1–B.2).

sections.title: Binary ↔ Decimal Conversion
sections.description: Place value in base 2, and the two conversion directions, worked step by step (LO DAT-1.C).

sections.title: Deep Dive: Hexadecimal
sections.description: Why programmers write bits in base 16 — a compact shorthand for long binary strings.

slides.note: Fill in your Guided Notes as we go. The Topic 2.1 page on apcsexamprep.com has every answer if you fall behind.
slides.script: Here's the map for today's Guided Notes — four sections. The first three are green, CB-required: representing data with bits, the cost of finite bits, and the conversion skill. The fourth is a blue deep-dive on hexadecimal — not required by the College Board, but it makes you faster at reading binary. Keep the packet open and fill it in as we go; if you fall behind, every answer is on the Topic 2.1 page.

slides.label: Representing Data with Bits
slides.kicker: Binary and Data Abstraction · Learning Objective DAT-1.A
slides.script: Section one. Before we can talk about compressing data, analyzing data, or protecting data — all coming later in this Big Idea — we have to nail down what data physically is inside the machine. The answer is shockingly simple, and the College Board is precise about the vocabulary.

slides.heading: Key Vocabulary (LO DAT-1.A)

terms.definition: The smallest unit of data a computer stores — a single binary digit, either 0 or 1 (EK DAT-1.A.1).
A group of 8 bits — the standard chunk size for measuring data (EK DAT-1.A.1).
Grouping bits to represent something more meaningful — numbers, characters, colors — so you can think about the meaning instead of the raw 0s and 1s (EK DAT-1.A.2).
Data with values that change smoothly and continuously, like sound waves, temperature, or the position of a spinning dial (EK DAT-1.A.4).
Measuring an analog signal at regular intervals and storing each measurement as bits — how continuous reality is approximated digitally (EK DAT-1.A.4).

slides.script: Five words that carry this whole section. A bit is the smallest unit of data — one binary digit, 0 or 1. Eight of them make a byte. Abstraction is the big idea hiding in plain sight: bits get grouped to represent numbers, characters, colors — so you can work with the meaning and ignore the raw digits. Analog data is anything that changes smoothly, like a sound wave. And sampling is how we get analog reality into a digital machine: measure at regular intervals, store each measurement as bits. The more often you sample, the closer the digital copy gets to the smooth original.

slides.heading: One Alphabet, Every Kind of Data
bullets: Bits are grouped to represent abstractions — numbers, characters, colors, sound, and more (EK DAT-1.A.2).
The SAME sequence of bits can represent different data in different contexts: 01000001 is the number 65, the letter 'A', or a shade in an image — the context decides (EK DAT-1.A.3).
There is no special 'photo storage' or 'music storage' inside a computer — every file is bits, and the file format is the contract for how to interpret them.
This is why opening a file with the wrong program produces garbage: the bits are fine, the interpretation is wrong.
slides.script: Here's the idea students remember for years. A computer has exactly one alphabet: 0 and 1. Numbers, letters, colors, songs — all of them are bits, just grouped and interpreted differently. And the College Board makes a specific point of the flip side: the same sequence of bits means different things in different contexts. The pattern 01000001 is the number 65 if you read it as a number, the letter capital-A if you read it as text, and a color value if it lands inside an image. Nothing in the bits themselves announces what they are — the context, the file format, is the contract. That's also why opening a file in the wrong program shows garbage: the bits survived, the interpretation didn't.

slides.heading: Analog World, Digital Machine
slides.subheading: Real-world signals change smoothly; computers store discrete bits. Sampling is the bridge (EK DAT-1.A.4).

left.title: Analog
left.definition: Values change smoothly, with infinitely many in-between values
examples: A sound wave rising and falling continuously
A mercury thermometer sliding between marks
A dimmer knob with no clicks — every position is a value

right.title: Digital (sampled)
right.definition: The analog signal is measured at regular intervals; each measurement becomes bits
examples: An audio file storing thousands of measurements per second
A digital thermometer reporting 72.4 — one number per reading
More frequent samples ⇒ closer approximation of the smooth original

slides.footer: AP TIP: If a question says a real-world signal is 'measured at regular intervals,' it is describing sampling — the analog-to-digital bridge.
slides.script: Two columns, one bridge. The real world is analog: sound waves, temperature, brightness all change smoothly with infinitely many in-between values. A computer can't store infinity — it stores bits. Sampling is the bridge: measure the analog signal at regular intervals and store each measurement as a binary number. A song file is just thousands of those measurements per second, played back fast enough that your ear hears a smooth wave. And the trade-off is intuitive: sample more often and the digital copy hugs the original more closely, at the cost of more bits. On the exam, the phrase 'measured at regular intervals' is the tell — that's sampling.

slides.heading: “The Computer Stores a Tiny Picture”
slides.misconception: Images, sounds, and videos are stored as miniature pictures and recordings inside the computer — only numbers are stored 'in binary.'
slides.whyItHappens: We see thumbnails and hear playback, so it feels like the media itself lives in the machine. The binary layer is invisible at every level users touch.
slides.correction: EVERYTHING is stored as bits — there is no other storage. An image is a grid of numbers (pixel colors), sound is a list of numbers (samples), and text is numbers (character codes), all encoded in binary. The 'picture' only exists when software interprets those bits (EK DAT-1.A.2, DAT-1.A.3).
slides.example: Rename a .png file to .txt and open it: you'll see pages of gibberish characters. Same bits, wrong interpretation — the picture was never 'in there' as a picture.
slides.script: Here's the trap. Because we see thumbnails and hear playback, it's natural to imagine tiny pictures and recordings living inside the machine, with binary reserved for math. But there is only one kind of storage: bits. An image is a grid of pixel-color numbers. A song is a long list of sample numbers. Text is character-code numbers. The picture appears only at the moment software interprets the bits as a picture. The rename experiment proves it: change a png extension to txt and open it — pages of gibberish. Same bits, wrong contract.

slides.heading: Stop and Think — Independent Writing
slides.subheading: These map to LO DAT-1.A. Complete on your Guided Notes.
prompts: A friend claims their computer 'stores photos differently from numbers.' In two sentences, correct them using the word abstraction.
The bit sequence 01000001 shows up in two different files. In one it displays as 'A'; in the other it contributes to a pixel. Explain how both can be true.
A vinyl record is analog; a streaming file is digital. Describe what sampling does in the trip from one to the other.
slides.script: Stop and think — independent, six minutes, full sentences on your Guided Notes. One: correct the friend who thinks photos and numbers are stored differently, and use the word abstraction while you do it. Two: explain how the exact same bit sequence can be a letter in one file and part of a pixel in another. Three: trace the trip from vinyl to streaming file and say precisely what sampling contributes. These are the three moves DAT-1.A questions make on the exam.

slides.label: The Cost of Finite Bits
slides.kicker: Overflow and Round-off · Learning Objective DAT-1.B
slides.script: Section two. Your bell-ringer scheme broke in two places: it couldn't hold 300, and it couldn't hold 72.5. Those weren't flaws in your design — they are the two consequences the College Board names for ALL binary representation. Every computer that has ever existed pays these same two costs.

slides.heading: Two Failure Modes, One Root Cause
slides.subheading: A fixed number of bits can only represent a fixed set of values (EK DAT-1.B.1, DAT-1.B.2).

left.title: Integer overflow
left.definition: The value is too LARGE for the bits available
examples: Fixed bit-width sets a hard maximum — 8 bits tops out at 255
Push past the max and the value wraps or errors — it cannot 'stretch'
Your 8 switches couldn't send 300 — that was overflow

right.title: Round-off error
right.definition: The value is too PRECISE for the bits available
examples: Real numbers are stored as floating-point approximations
Many values (like 0.1) can't be stored exactly in binary — the stored value is merely close
Your switches couldn't send 72.5 — that was a precision limit

slides.footer: AP TIP: Overflow = too BIG for the bits. Round-off = too PRECISE for the bits. Name the failure mode by asking which limit was hit.
slides.script: Both failure modes come from one root cause: a fixed number of bits can only represent a fixed set of values. Integer overflow is the too-big failure: with 8 bits the ceiling is 255, and pushing past it wraps around or errors out — the bits cannot stretch. Round-off error is the too-precise failure: real numbers are stored as floating-point approximations, and lots of ordinary values, like one-tenth, have no exact binary form, so the machine stores something merely close. Your bell-ringer scheme hit both walls — 300 was overflow, 72.5 was precision. On the exam, diagnose by asking which limit got hit: size or precision.

slides.heading: Finite Bits in the Wild
slides.context: Same two failure modes, four real scenarios. For each: which consequence is it?
rows: A game stores your score in 8 bits; at 255 the next point shows 0
Value exceeded the maximum the bits can hold and wrapped
0.1 and 0.2 have no exact binary form; tiny errors accumulated
A 2014 video's view counter froze at 2,147,483,647 until the site widened it
The count hit the maximum of its fixed bit-width
A physics program's repeated calculations drift slightly from the exact answer
Each floating-point step stored an approximation
slides.script: Four real scenarios, same two diagnoses. The 8-bit game score wrapping from 255 to 0 — overflow, the value exceeded what the bits can hold. The famous 0.1 plus 0.2 giving 0.30000000000000004 — round-off, because neither 0.1 nor 0.2 has an exact binary form, and the tiny errors show up in the sum. The video view counter that froze at 2,147,483,647 — that number is the ceiling of a common fixed bit-width, pure overflow, and the site had to widen the storage. And a physics simulation drifting over thousands of steps — accumulated round-off. Notice the diagnosis never requires knowing the programming language: just ask which limit was hit.

slides.heading: Stop and Think — Diagnose the Failure
slides.subheading: These map to LO DAT-1.B. Complete on your Guided Notes.
prompts: A fitness tracker stores daily steps in 16 bits (max 65,535). An ultramarathoner logs 70,000 steps and the app shows 4,464. Name the consequence and explain the number shown.
A banking app computes interest and is off by a fraction of a cent after millions of accounts. Which consequence is this, and why does it grow with repetition?
In one sentence: what single fact about binary representation causes BOTH failure modes?
slides.script: Three diagnostics, five minutes, full sentences. One: the fitness tracker showing 4,464 after 70,000 steps — name the consequence and explain exactly where 4,464 comes from; it's 70,000 minus 65,536, which is the wrap. Two: the banking app off by a fraction of a cent across millions of accounts — say which failure it is and why repetition makes it grow. Three: compress the whole section into one sentence — what single fact about binary storage causes both failures? The sentence you want involves the word fixed.

slides.label: Binary ↔ Decimal Conversion
slides.kicker: The Tested Skill · Learning Objective DAT-1.C
slides.script: Section three, the hands-on skill. The College Board tests conversion in both directions: given a binary number, find the decimal value; given a decimal number, write it in binary. It is pure mechanics once you see that binary works exactly like the number system you already know — just with a different base.

slides.heading: Place Value: The Only Rule You Need
bullets: A number base defines how numbers are written: decimal is base 10 (digits 0–9), binary is base 2 (digits 0–1) (EK DAT-1.C.1).
In ANY base, each position is worth the base raised to the power of that position, counting from 0 on the right.
Decimal 234 = 2×100 + 3×10 + 4×1 — powers of ten. You've used place value your whole life.
Binary works identically with powers of two: the positions are worth 1, 2, 4, 8, 16, 32, 64, 128, ...
slides.script: One rule powers everything: place value. In decimal, 234 means two hundreds plus three tens plus four ones — each position worth a power of ten. Binary is the identical system with base 2: each position is worth a power of two, so reading right to left the positions are worth 1, 2, 4, 8, 16, 32, 64, 128, and so on. Every conversion problem on the exam is just this rule applied in one direction or the other. If you can read 234 as hundreds-tens-ones, you already own the concept — we're only swapping the base.

slides.heading: Both Directions, Worked
slides.context: Direction 1: binary → decimal (add the place values under each 1). Direction 2: decimal → binary (subtract the largest power of 2 that fits, repeat).
rows: Write place values under the bits:  8 4 2 1
Largest power of 2 ≤ 22 is 16 → write a 1 there
Keep the values under each 1:  8, 4, and 1
slides.script: Both directions, side by side. Binary to decimal is the easy direction: write the place values 8-4-2-1 under the bits of 1101, keep the values sitting under each 1 — that's 8, 4, and 1 — skip the value under the 0, and add: thirteen. Decimal to binary runs the same logic backward as repeated subtraction: for 22, the largest power of two that fits is 16, take it and mark that place with a 1; 22 minus 16 leaves 6, the largest power that fits is 4, mark it; 6 minus 4 leaves 2, mark the 2s place; nothing remains, so every unused place — the 8s and the 1s — gets a 0. Read it off: 10110. Two directions, one rule.

slides.heading: “Binary 11 Is Eleven”
slides.misconception: You read a binary number by pronouncing its digits as if they were decimal — so 11 is 'eleven' and 101 is 'one hundred one.'
slides.whyItHappens: The digits LOOK identical to decimal digits, and a decade of reading base 10 makes the decimal reading automatic.
slides.correction: The digits only have meaning relative to their base. Binary 11 is 2+1 = 3, and binary 101 is 4+0+1 = 5. Always run the place values — never pronounce binary as decimal (EK DAT-1.C.1).
slides.example: Exam distractors are built from exactly this reflex: for binary 101, the wrong answers will include 101 ('one hundred one') and 6 (adjacent value) — the right answer, 5, requires the place-value habit.
slides.script: The classic trap, and exam writers know it. Binary digits look exactly like decimal digits, so ten years of reading base 10 makes you want to call binary 11 'eleven.' It's three: a 2 plus a 1. Binary 101 isn't one hundred one, it's five: a 4, no 2, and a 1. The fix is a habit, not a fact — every time you meet a binary number, physically write the place values underneath before you do anything else. Multiple-choice distractors are engineered for the students who skip that step.

slides.heading: Deep Dive: Why Programmers Love Base 16
slides.subheading: Hexadecimal isn't required by the CED — but it's everywhere in real computing, and it makes long binary readable.

left.title: The problem with raw binary
left.definition: Real values need LOTS of bits, and humans lose their place
examples: Reading or copying that without error is nearly impossible
We need a compact, lossless shorthand

right.title: The hexadecimal fix
right.definition: Base 16: one hex digit (0–9, A–F) stands for exactly 4 bits
examples: That same color collapses to FF0080
You've seen this: #FF0080 is a web color code
It's still the same bits — just grouped 4 at a time for human eyes

slides.footer: Every #RRGGBB color code you've ever seen in a web page is hexadecimal-wrapped binary.
slides.script: A deep dive for context — the College Board doesn't require hexadecimal, but you will meet it constantly in real computing. The problem: real values need lots of bits. A single screen color is 24 of them, and no human can read twenty-four 0s and 1s without losing their place. The fix: base 16, where one digit — 0 through 9, then A through F — stands for exactly four bits. That unreadable 24-bit color collapses to FF0080, and suddenly the web color codes you've seen in every style sheet make sense: a hash sign followed by hex-wrapped binary. Same bits, grouped for human eyes.

slides.heading: Stop and Think — Convert Both Ways
slides.subheading: These map to LO DAT-1.C. Show your place values on your Guided Notes.
prompts: Convert binary 10110 to decimal. Write the place values under the bits before you add.
Convert decimal 41 to binary using repeated subtraction of powers of 2. Show each subtraction.
A classmate says binary 1000 is 'one thousand.' Give the correct value and name the habit that prevents this mistake.
slides.script: Conversion practice, six minutes, and the steps matter more than the answers. One: binary 10110 to decimal — place values first, then add; you should land on 22, which you saw built in the worked table. Two: decimal 41 to binary by repeated subtraction — 32 fits, then 8, then 1, giving 101001. Three: the classmate calling binary 1000 'one thousand' — it's eight, and the habit that prevents the mistake is writing place values under every binary number before reading it. Answers are reviewed as a class before the exit.

slides.heading: Binary Numbers, in One Slide
points: Computers store exactly one thing: bits. Grouped bits represent numbers, text, colors, sound — abstraction (EK DAT-1.A.1–A.2).
The same bits mean different things in different contexts — the file format is the contract (EK DAT-1.A.3).
Analog signals enter the digital world by sampling: measurements at regular intervals (EK DAT-1.A.4).
Fixed bits ⇒ two failure modes: overflow (too big) and round-off (too precise) (EK DAT-1.B.1–B.2).
Conversion is place value: powers of 2 under the bits, add under the 1s; reverse with repeated subtraction (LO DAT-1.C).
slides.next: Topic 2.2, Data Compression: now that everything is bits, how do we use FEWER of them? Lossless vs. lossy — and what you're willing to throw away.
slides.script: The whole topic in one slide. Computers store bits, and grouped bits represent everything — that's abstraction. The same bits mean different things in different contexts, so the format is the contract. Analog reality gets in by sampling. A fixed number of bits buys you two failure modes: overflow when a value is too big, round-off when it's too precise. And conversion is nothing but place value run in either direction. Next class is Topic 2.2, Data Compression — now that everything is bits, the question becomes how to use fewer of them, and what you're willing to throw away to get there. Guided Notes are collected at the end of class.

quiz.title: Topic 2.1 — Binary Numbers — Quiz
quiz.instructions: Choose the single best answer. 8 questions, 15 minutes, no notes.

questions.stem: A student opens an image file in a text editor and sees pages of random-looking characters instead of the photo. Per EK DAT-1.A.3, what best explains this?
options: The file was corrupted when it was renamed, destroying the original photo data
The same sequence of bits represents different data in different contexts, and the editor applied a text interpretation
Image files contain hidden text that only appears when the image cannot be displayed
The text editor converted the image's pixels into characters to save storage space
questions.rationale: EK DAT-1.A.3 states that the same sequence of bits may represent different types of data in different contexts. The bits are unchanged; the text editor simply interpreted image bits under a text contract, producing gibberish characters. A is wrong because nothing was corrupted — the image still opens in an image viewer. C invents hidden text that does not exist. D describes a conversion that never happened; editors interpret bits, they do not transform the file.

questions.stem: Per EK DAT-1.A.1, which statement correctly describes the most basic unit of data in a computer?
options: A byte, which can hold any single character of text
A pixel, which stores one point of color information
A bit, a binary digit that can be either 0 or 1
A word, which is the width of the computer's processor
questions.rationale: EK DAT-1.A.1 defines the bit — a binary digit with value 0 or 1 — as the smallest unit of data. A byte (option A) is a group of 8 bits, so it is not the most basic unit. A pixel (B) is an abstraction built from many bits. A word (D) is a hardware grouping, larger still. Only the bit is fundamental.

questions.stem: A microphone captures a smooth, continuously varying sound wave, and software stores 44,100 numeric measurements of it per second. Per EK DAT-1.A.4, this process is best described as:
options: Compression of the sound wave into a smaller audio file
Integer overflow of the values the sound wave contains
Abstraction of the sound into a differently named file type
Sampling of the analog signal at regular intervals
questions.rationale: EK DAT-1.A.4 describes analog data as smoothly varying and explains that it is approximated digitally by measuring at regular intervals — sampling. 44,100 measurements per second is a sampling rate. A is wrong because compression reduces already-digital data. B misuses overflow, which is about fixed-width integer limits. C stretches abstraction past its meaning; the defining feature here is interval measurement, which the EK names sampling.

questions.stem: A game stores the player's coin count using a fixed number of bits with a maximum value of 255. A player collects one more coin at 255, and the display shows 0. Per EK DAT-1.B.1, this is an example of:
options: Integer overflow — the value exceeded the range its fixed number of bits can represent
Round-off error — the coin count was stored as an imprecise approximation
Sampling error — the game measured the coin count at too coarse an interval
Data abstraction — the game grouped the coin bits into a different meaning
questions.rationale: EK DAT-1.B.1 states that fixed-width integer storage limits the range of values, and exceeding it causes overflow — here the count wrapped past its 255 maximum back to 0. B is the wrong failure mode: round-off concerns precision of real numbers, and a coin count is a whole number. C misapplies sampling, which concerns analog signals. D misuses abstraction, which is about representation, not failure.

questions.stem: A program adds 0.1 + 0.2 and prints 0.30000000000000004. Per EK DAT-1.B.2, why does this happen?
options: The program exceeded the maximum range of integer storage
The keyboard input was misread as slightly different numbers
Real numbers are stored as floating-point approximations, and many values have no exact binary form
Decimal values must be rounded to whole numbers before use
questions.rationale: EK DAT-1.B.2 explains that real numbers are represented as floating-point approximations, which produces round-off errors. Neither 0.1 nor 0.2 has an exact binary representation, so the machine stores close approximations whose sum is visibly off. A names the wrong failure mode — nothing exceeded a range. B invents an input problem. D is false — programs handle decimal values constantly, as approximations.

questions.stem: What is the decimal (base 10) value of the binary number 1101?
questions.rationale: Place values right to left are 1, 2, 4, 8. The bits of 1101 put 1s over the 8, the 4, and the 1 (the 2s place holds a 0), so the value is 8 + 4 + 1 = 13. B is the classic read-it-as-decimal trap. C invents an operation. D wrongly skips the 4s place, which holds a 1.

questions.stem: A sensor must transmit the decimal value 22 in binary. Which bit sequence should it send?
options: 11010, which places 1s in the 16s, 8s, and 2s positions
10110, which places 1s in the 16s, 4s, and 2s positions
10101, which places 1s in the 16s, 4s, and 1s positions
11100, which places 1s in the 16s, 8s, and 4s positions
questions.rationale: Repeated subtraction: 22 − 16 = 6, then 6 − 4 = 2, then 2 − 2 = 0, so the 16s, 4s, and 2s places hold 1s: 10110. Option A sums to 26, B sums to 21, and D sums to 28 — each is one wrong subtraction step away from the correct answer, which is exactly how these distractors are built.

questions.stem: Per EK DAT-1.A.2, grouped bits can represent which of the following? I. Numbers. II. Characters of text. III. Colors in an image.
options: I only
I and II only
II and III only
I, II, and III
questions.rationale: EK DAT-1.A.2 states that bits are grouped to represent abstractions including, but not limited to, numbers, characters, and colors — all three items are named by the EK itself. A reflects the misconception that binary is only for math. B wrongly claims colors need analog storage. C is doubly wrong: numbers are stored in binary, and item I clearly belongs.

guide.title: Topic 2.1 — Binary Numbers — Teacher Guide
guide.subtitle: Big Idea 2: Data · Topic 2.1 · 1 class period + assessment block
overview: Topic 2.1 opens Big Idea 2 with the physical truth underneath all of computing: every kind of data is stored as bits. Students learn the representation story (bits, abstraction, context-dependence, sampling), the two costs of finite representation (integer overflow and round-off error), and the mechanical skill the exam tests directly — converting positive integers between binary and decimal in both directions.

The through-line is the bell ringer: students design an 8-switch scheme to transmit a temperature, then discover their design already contains binary representation and both of its named failure modes.
los: LO DAT-1.A — Explain how data can be represented using bits.
LO DAT-1.B — Explain the consequences of using bits to represent data.
LO DAT-1.C — For binary numbers: calculate the binary (base 2) equivalent of a positive integer (base 10), and vice versa.
eks: EK DAT-1.A.1 — The smallest unit of data in a computer is a bit (binary digit), which can be either 0 or 1; a byte is a group of 8 bits.
EK DAT-1.A.2 — Bits are grouped to represent abstractions, including but not limited to numbers, characters, and colors.
EK DAT-1.A.3 — The same sequence of bits may represent different types of data in different contexts.
EK DAT-1.A.4 — Analog data have values that change smoothly; analog data can be approximated digitally by sampling — measuring values at regular intervals.
EK DAT-1.B.1 — In many programming languages, integers are represented by a fixed number of bits, which limits their range; values exceeding the range cause integer overflow.
EK DAT-1.B.2 — Real numbers are approximated by floating-point representations, which can result in round-off errors.
EK DAT-1.C.1 — Number bases, including binary (base 2) and decimal (base 10), define how numbers are represented; the value of each position is the base raised to the power of the position.
materials: Printed Topic 2.1 quiz (student copies) for the assessment block.
Optional: 8 physical switches, coins, or cards for the bell-ringer demonstration.
prereqs: None within Big Idea 2 — this is its opening topic. Comfort with decimal place value (hundreds / tens / ones) is assumed and is explicitly activated in Section 3.

pacing.focus: Represent data with bits; name the two consequences; convert binary ↔ decimal (LO DAT-1.A, DAT-1.B, DAT-1.C)

segments.segment: Representing data with bits: vocab, abstraction, context, sampling (EK DAT-1.A.1–A.4)
The cost of finite bits: overflow vs round-off + real cases (EK DAT-1.B.1–B.2)
Stop and Think diagnostics on DAT-1.B
Conversion both directions: place value + worked table + trap (LO DAT-1.C; hex deep-dive optional)

pacing.label: Flex / Assessment block — no new deck (uses the Topic Quiz)
pacing.focus: Not a numbered prep day. Run the Topic 2.1 quiz as the opener of the next class (doubles as spaced retrieval) or as a same-day exit assessment if the period allows. The quiz ships as its own document, so no new deck or notes are needed.

misconceptions.heading: “The computer stores a tiny picture”
misconceptions.myth: Media files are stored as miniature pictures and recordings; only numbers are stored in binary.
misconceptions.reality: Everything is bits. Images are grids of pixel-color numbers, sound is a list of sample numbers, text is character codes — the picture exists only when software interprets the bits. The rename-a-png-to-txt demonstration makes this land.

misconceptions.heading: “Binary 11 is eleven”
misconceptions.myth: Binary numbers are read by pronouncing their digits as decimal.
misconceptions.reality: Digits only have meaning relative to their base: binary 11 is 3, binary 101 is 5. Enforce the habit of writing place values under every binary number — exam distractors are built from the decimal-reading reflex.

misconceptions.heading: “Overflow and round-off are the same bug”
misconceptions.myth: Both failures are just 'the computer ran out of space,' interchangeable on the exam.
misconceptions.reality: They are distinct, separately named consequences: overflow is a RANGE failure of fixed-width integers (value too big); round-off is a PRECISION failure of floating-point reals (value too exact to store). Exam items require choosing the right one.

support: Provide a place-value strip (128 64 32 16 8 4 2 1) students physically lay under any binary number before reading it.
For decimal→binary, give the sentence frame: 'The largest power of 2 that fits into ____ is ____, so a 1 goes in that place.'
extension: Have students determine the largest value representable in n bits and articulate the 2^n − 1 pattern from evidence.
Point deep-dive students at the hexadecimal slide, then have them decode a real #RRGGBB color from a website they use into its three decimal channel values.

discussion: Your phone, a supercomputer, and a 1970s calculator all store data the same way — as bits. What does that suggest about where the real differences between computers live?
Sampling throws away everything between measurements, yet music streaming sounds smooth. When is a lossy approximation of reality good enough — and who decides?
The view-counter overflow was fixed by adding more bits. Why doesn't 'just add more bits everywhere' eliminate overflow as a category of failure?
guide.answerKeyNote: Each question's full rationale is on the KEY copy of the quiz (print it with the answer-key option). The short answer key below is auto-derived from the live quiz block, so it can never drift from the student questions.
